51	---
52	
53	## Contributions & Communication
54	
55	Contributions and communications are expected to occur through:
56	
57	- [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/entireio/cli/issues) - Bug reports and feature requests
58	- [Discord](https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S) - Questions, general conversation, and real-time support
59	
60	Please represent the project and community respectfully in all public and private interactions.
61	
62	
63	## How to Contribute
64	
65	
66	There are many ways to contribute:
67	
68	- **Feature requests** - Open a [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/entireio/cli/issues) to discuss your idea
69	- **Bug reports** - Report issues via [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/entireio/cli/issues) (see [Reporting Bugs](#reporting-bugs))
70	- **Code contributions** - Fix bugs, add features, improve tests
71	- **Documentation** - Improve guides, fix typos, add examples
72	- **Community** - Help others, answer questions, share knowledge
73	
74	
75	## Reporting Bugs
76	
77	Good bug reports help us fix issues quickly. When reporting a bug, please include:
78	
79	### Required Information
80	
81	1. **Entire CLI version** - run `entire version`
82	2. **Operating system**
83	3. **Go version** - run `go version`
84	
85	### What to Include
86	
87	Please answer these questions in your bug report:
88	
89	1. **What did you do?** - Include the exact commands you ran
90	2. **What did you expect to happen?**
91	3. **What actually happened?** - Include the full error message or unexpected output
92	4. **Can you reproduce it?** - Does it happen every time or intermittently?
93	5. **Any additional context?** - Logs, screenshots, or related issues
94	
95	---
96	
97	## Local Setup
98	
99	### Prerequisites
100	
101	- **Go 1.26.x** - Check with `go version`
102	- **mise** - Task runner and version manager. Install with `curl https://mise.run | sh`
103	
104	### Clone and Install
105	
106	```bash
107	# Clone the repository
108	git clone https://github.com/entireio/cli.git
109	cd cli
110	
111	# Trust the mise configuration (required on first setup)
112	mise trust
113	
114	# Install dependencies (mise will install the correct Go version)
115	mise install
116	
117	# Download Go modules
118	go mod download
119	
120	# Build the CLI
121	mise run build
122	
123	# Verify setup by running tests
124	mise run test
125	```
126	
127	> See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for detailed architecture and development reference.
128	
129	---
130	
131	## Making Changes
132	
133	1. **Create a branch** for your changes:
134	   ```bash
135	   git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name
136	   ```
137	
138	2. **Make your changes** - follow the [Code Style](#code-style) guidelines
139	
140	3. **Test your changes** - see [Testing](#testing)
141	
142	4. **Commit** with clear, descriptive messages:
143	   ```bash
144	   git commit -m "Add feature: description of what you added"
145	   ```
146	
147	---
148	
149	## Code Style
150	
151	Follow standard Go idioms and conventions. For detailed guidance, see the **Go Code Style** section in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md).
152	
153	### Key Points
154	
155	- **Error handling**: Handle all errors explicitly - don't leave them unchecked
156	- **Formatting**: Code must pass `gofmt` (run `mise run fmt`)
157	- **Linting**: Code must pass `golangci-lint` (run `mise run lint`)
158	- **Naming**: Use meaningful, descriptive names following Go conventions
159	
160	---
161	
162	## Testing
163	
164	> See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for complete testing documentation.
165	
166	```bash
167	# Unit tests - always run before committing
168	mise run test
169	
170	# Integration tests
171	mise run test:integration
172	
173	# Full CI suite
174	mise run test:ci
175	```
176	
177	Integration tests use the `//go:build integration` build tag and are located in `cmd/entire/cli/integration_test/`.
178	
179	---
180	
181	## Creating an Agent
182	
183	
184	Entire supports two ways to create agents:
185	
186	### 1. Claude Code Agent Personas (Markdown)
187	
188	These are markdown files that define specialized behaviors for Claude Code (e.g., developer, reviewer, etc.).
189	
190	- **Location:** `.claude/agents/`
191	- **Structure:**
192	   ```markdown
193	   ---
194	   name: my-agent
195	   description: What this agent does
196	   model: opus
197	   color: blue
198	   ---
199	
200	   # Agent Name
201	   You are a **[Role]** with expertise in [domain].
202	
203	   ## Core Principles
204	   - Principle 1
205	   - Principle 2
206	
207	   ## Process
208	   1. Step 1
209	   2. Step 2
210	
211	   ## Output Format
212	   How to structure responses...
213	   ```
214	- **To invoke:** Create a matching command in `.claude/commands/` that spawns the agent via the Task tool.
215	- **Examples:**
216	   - `.claude/agents/dev.md` - TDD Developer
217	   - `.claude/agents/reviewer.md` - Code Reviewer
218	
219	### 2. Coding Agent Integrations (Go)
220	
221	These are Go implementations that integrate Entire with different AI coding tools (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, Cursor, Factory AI Droid, Copilot CLI, etc.) using the Agent abstraction layer.
222	
223	- **Location:** `cmd/entire/cli/agent/`
224	- **Steps:**
225	   1. Implement the `Agent` interface in `agent/agent.go`
226	   2. Register your agent in the agent registry
227	   3. Add setup and hook configuration as needed
228	   4. Ensure session and checkpoint tracking is handled per the abstraction
229	- **Reference:** See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for architecture and code examples.
230	
231	---
232	
233	**Which should I use?**
234	
235	- Use a persona markdown agent if you want to create a new role or workflow for Claude Code.
236	- Use a coding agent integration if you want to add support for a new AI coding tool or extend agent capabilities in the CLI.
237	
238	---
239	
240	## Submitting a Pull Request
241	
242	### Before You Submit
243	
244	- **Related issue exists and is approved** -- Your PR references an issue where a maintainer has acknowledged the approach. (Exceptions: documentation fixes, typo corrections, and `good-first-issue` items.)
245	- **Linting passes** -- Run `mise run lint` (includes golangci-lint, gofmt, gomod, shellcheck)
246	- **Tests pass** -- Run `mise run test` to verify your changes
247	- **Tests included** -- New Go code and functionality should have accompanying tests
248	- **Entire checkpoint trailers included** -- See [Using Entire While Contributing](#using-entire-while-contributing) below
249	
250	PRs that skip these steps are likely to be closed without merge.
251	
252	### Submitting
253	
254	1. **Push** your branch to your fork
255	2. **Open a PR** against the `main` branch
256	3. **Describe your changes** -- Link the related issue, summarize what changed and what testing you did
257	4. **Address Copilot feedback** -- See [Responding to Automated Review](#responding-to-automated-review)
258	5. **Wait for maintainer review**
259	
260	---
261	
262	## Responding to Automated Review
263	
264	Co-pilot agent reviews every PR and provides feedback on code quality, potential bugs, and project conventions.
265	
266	**Read and respond to every Copilot comment.** PRs with unaddressed Copilot feedback will not move to maintainer review.
267	
268	- **Fixed** -- Push a commit addressing the issue.
269	- **Disagree** -- Reply explaining your reasoning. The Copilot isn't always right.
270	- **Question** -- Ask for clarification. We're happy to help.
271	
272	Addressing Copilot feedback upfront is the fastest path to maintainer review.
273	
274	---
275	
276	## Using Entire While Contributing
277	
278	We use Entire on Entire. When contributing, install the Entire CLI and let it capture your coding sessions -- this gives us valuable dogfooding data and helps improve the tool.
279	
280	### Setup
281	
282	Install the latest version of the Entire CLI (see [installation docs](https://docs.entire.io/cli/installation)) and verify with `entire version`. Entire is already configured in this repository, so there's no need to run `entire enable`.
283	
284	### Checkpoint Trailers
285	
286	All commits should include `Entire-Checkpoint` trailers from your sessions. These are added automatically by the `prepare-commit-msg` hook when Entire is enabled. The trailers link your commits to session metadata on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch.
287	
288	### Sessions Branch
289	
290	When you push your PR branch, Entire can automatically push the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch alongside it (if `push_sessions` is enabled in your settings). Include this in your PR so maintainers can review the session context behind your changes.
291	
292	---
293	
294	## Troubleshooting
295	
296	### Common Setup Issues
297	
298	**`go mod download` fails with timeout**
299	```bash
300	# Try using direct mode
301	GOPROXY=direct go mod download
302	```
303	
304	**`mise install` fails**
305	```bash
306	# Ensure mise is properly installed
307	curl https://mise.run | sh
308	
309	# Reload your shell
310	source ~/.zshrc  # or ~/.bashrc
311	```
312	
313	**Binary not updating after rebuild**
314	```bash
315	# Check which binary is being used
316	which entire
317	type -a entire
318	
319	# You may have multiple installations - update the correct path
320	```
321	
322	---
323	
324	## Community
325	
326	Join the Entire community:
327	
328	- **Discord** - [Join our server][discord] for discussions and support
329	
330	[discord]: https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S
331	
332	---
333	
334	## Additional Resources
335	
336	- [README](README.md) - Setup and usage documentation
337	- [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) - Architecture and development reference (Claude Code)
338	- [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) - Architecture and development reference (Gemini CLI, OpenCode, Cursor, Factory AI Droid, Copilot CLI)
339	- [Code of Conduct](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) - Community guidelines
340	- [Security Policy](SECURITY.md) - Reporting security vulnerabilities
341	
342	---
343	
344	Thank you for contributing!

input.content:
# Contributing to git-sync

Thank you for your interest in contributing to Entire! We welcome contributions from everyone.

Please read our [Code of Conduct](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) before participating.

> **New to Entire?** See the [README](README.md) for setup and usage documentation.

---

## Before You Code: Discuss First

The fastest way to get a contribution merged is to align with maintainers before writing code. Please **open an issue first** using our [issue templates](https://github.com/entireio/git-sync/issues/new/choose) and wait for maintainer feedback before starting implementation.

### Contribution Workflow

1. **Open an issue** describing the problem or feature
2. **Wait for maintainer feedback** -- we may have relevant context or plans
3. **Get approval** before starting implementation
4. **Submit your PR** referencing the approved issue
5. **Address all feedback** including automated Copilot comments
6. **Maintainer review and merge**

---

## First-Time Contributors

New to the project? Welcome! Here's how to get started:

### Good First Issues

We recommend starting with:
- **Documentation improvements** - Fix typos, clarify explanations, add examples
- **Test contributions** - Add test cases, improve coverage
- **Small bug fixes** - Issues labeled `good-first-issue`

---

## Submitting Issues

All feature requests, bug reports, and general issues should be submitted through [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/entireio/git-sync/issues). Please search for existing issues before opening a new one.

For security-related issues, see the Security section below.

---

## Security

If you discover a security vulnerability, **do not report it through GitHub Issues**. Instead, please follow the instructions in our [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md) file for responsible disclosure. All security reports are kept confidential as described in SECURITY.md.

---

## Contributions & Communication

Contributions and communications are expected to occur through:

- [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/entireio/git-sync/issues) - Bug reports and feature requests
- [Discord](https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S) - Questions, general conversation, and real-time support

Please represent the project and community respectfully in all public and private interactions.


## How to Contribute


There are many ways to contribute:

- **Feature requests** - Open a [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/entireio/git-sync/issues) to discuss your idea
- **Bug reports** - Report issues via [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/entireio/git-sync/issues) (see [Reporting Bugs](#reporting-bugs))
- **Code contributions** - Fix bugs, add features, improve tests
- **Documentation** - Improve guides, fix typos, add examples
- **Community** - Help others, answer questions, share knowledge


## Reporting Bugs

Good bug reports help us fix issues quickly. When reporting a bug, please include:

### Required Information

1. **git-sync version** - run `git-sync version`
2. **Operating system**
3. **Go version** - run `go version`

### What to Include

Please answer these questions in your bug report:

1. **What did you do?** - Include the exact commands you ran
2. **What did you expect to happen?**
3. **What actually happened?** - Include the full error message or unexpected output
4. **Can you reproduce it?** - Does it happen every time or intermittently?
5. **Any additional context?** - Logs, screenshots, or related issues

---

## Local Setup

### Prerequisites

- **Go 1.26.x** - Check with `go version`
- **mise** - Task runner and version manager. Install with `curl https://mise.run | sh`

### Clone and Install

```bash
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/entireio/git-sync.git
cd git-sync

# Trust the mise configuration (required on first setup)
mise trust

# Install dependencies (mise will install the correct Go version)
mise install

# Download Go modules
go mod download

# Build the CLI
mise run build

# Verify setup by running tests
mise run test
```

> See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for detailed architecture and development reference.

---

## Making Changes

1. **Create a branch** for your changes:
   ```bash
   git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name
   ```

2. **Make your changes** - follow the [Code Style](#code-style) guidelines

3. **Test your changes** - see [Testing](#testing)

4. **Commit** with clear, descriptive messages:
   ```bash
   git commit -m "Add feature: description of what you added"
   ```

---

## Code Style

Follow standard Go idioms and conventions. For detailed guidance, see the **Go Code Style** section in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md).

### Key Points

- **Error handling**: Handle all errors explicitly - don't leave them unchecked
- **Formatting**: Code must pass `gofmt` (run `mise run fmt`)
- **Linting**: Code must pass `golangci-lint` (run `mise run lint`)
- **Naming**: Use meaningful, descriptive names following Go conventions

---

## Testing

> See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for complete testing documentation.

```bash
# Unit tests - always run before committing
mise run test

# Integration tests
mise run test:integration

# Full CI suite
mise run test:ci
```

Integration tests use the `//go:build integration` build tag and are located in `cmd/entire/cli/integration_test/`.

---

## Creating an Agent


Entire supports two ways to create agents:

### 1. Claude Code Agent Personas (Markdown)

These are markdown files that define specialized behaviors for Claude Code (e.g., developer, reviewer, etc.).

- **Location:** `.claude/agents/`
- **Structure:**
   ```markdown
   ---
   name: my-agent
   description: What this agent does
   model: opus
   color: blue
   ---

   # Agent Name
   You are a **[Role]** with expertise in [domain].

   ## Core Principles
   - Principle 1
   - Principle 2

   ## Process
   1. Step 1
   2. Step 2

   ## Output Format
   How to structure responses...
   ```
- **To invoke:** Create a matching command in `.claude/commands/` that spawns the agent via the Task tool.
- **Examples:**
   - `.claude/agents/dev.md` - TDD Developer
   - `.claude/agents/reviewer.md` - Code Reviewer

### 2. Coding Agent Integrations (Go)

These are Go implementations that integrate Entire with different AI coding tools (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, Cursor, Factory AI Droid, Copilot CLI, etc.) using the Agent abstraction layer.

- **Location:** `cmd/entire/cli/agent/`
- **Steps:**
   1. Implement the `Agent` interface in `agent/agent.go`
   2. Register your agent in the agent registry
   3. Add setup and hook configuration as needed
   4. Ensure session and checkpoint tracking is handled per the abstraction
- **Reference:** See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for architecture and code examples.

---

**Which should I use?**

- Use a persona markdown agent if you want to create a new role or workflow for Claude Code.
- Use a coding agent integration if you want to add support for a new AI coding tool or extend agent capabilities in the CLI.

---

## Submitting a Pull Request

### Before You Submit

- **Related issue exists and is approved** -- Your PR references an issue where a maintainer has acknowledged the approach. (Exceptions: documentation fixes, typo corrections, and `good-first-issue` items.)
- **Linting passes** -- Run `mise run lint` (includes golangci-lint, gofmt, gomod, shellcheck)
- **Tests pass** -- Run `mise run test` to verify your changes
- **Tests included** -- New Go code and functionality should have accompanying tests
- **Entire checkpoint trailers included** -- See [Using Entire While Contributing](#using-entire-while-contributing) below

PRs that skip these steps are likely to be closed without merge.

### Submitting

1. **Push** your branch to your fork
2. **Open a PR** against the `main` branch
3. **Describe your changes** -- Link the related issue, summarize what changed and what testing you did
4. **Address Copilot feedback** -- See [Responding to Automated Review](#responding-to-automated-review)
5. **Wait for maintainer review**

---

## Responding to Automated Review

Co-pilot agent reviews every PR and provides feedback on code quality, potential bugs, and project conventions.

**Read and respond to every Copilot comment.** PRs with unaddressed Copilot feedback will not move to maintainer review.

- **Fixed** -- Push a commit addressing the issue.
- **Disagree** -- Reply explaining your reasoning. The Copilot isn't always right.
- **Question** -- Ask for clarification. We're happy to help.

Addressing Copilot feedback upfront is the fastest path to maintainer review.

---

## Using Entire While Contributing

We use Entire on Entire. When contributing, install the Entire CLI and let it capture your coding sessions -- this gives us valuable dogfooding data and helps improve the tool.

### Setup

Install the latest version of the Entire CLI (see [installation docs](https://docs.entire.io/cli/installation)) and verify with `entire version`. Entire is already configured in this repository, so there's no need to run `entire enable`.

### Checkpoint Trailers

All commits should include `Entire-Checkpoint` trailers from your sessions. These are added automatically by the `prepare-commit-msg` hook when Entire is enabled. The trailers link your commits to session metadata on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch.

### Sessions Branch

When you push your PR branch, Entire can automatically push the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch alongside it (if `push_sessions` is enabled in your settings). Include this in your PR so maintainers can review the session context behind your changes.

---

## Troubleshooting

### Common Setup Issues

**`go mod download` fails with timeout**
```bash
# Try using direct mode
GOPROXY=direct go mod download
```

**`mise install` fails**
```bash
# Ensure mise is properly installed
curl https://mise.run | sh

# Reload your shell
source ~/.zshrc  # or ~/.bashrc
```

**Binary not updating after rebuild**
```bash
# Check which binary is being used
which git-sync
type -a git-sync

# You may have multiple installations - update the correct path
```

---

## Community

Join the Entire community:

- **Discord** - [Join our server][discord] for discussions and support

[discord]: https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S

---

## Additional Resources

- [README](README.md) - Setup and usage documentation
- [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) - Architecture and development reference (Claude Code)
- [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) - Architecture and development reference (Gemini CLI, OpenCode, Cursor, Factory AI Droid, Copilot CLI)
- [Code of Conduct](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) - Community guidelines
- [Security Policy](SECURITY.md) - Reporting security vulnerabilities

---

Thank you for contributing!

content.text:
Only the repo-name substitutions differ. CONTRIBUTING.md is now a 1:1 copy with that minimal divergence; CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md was already byte-identical.

The verbatim copy now re-includes `Creating an Agent`, `Using Entire While Contributing`, the `Submitting Issues` section, `Contributions & Communication`, and references to `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md`. Two of those references will resolve to missing files (no `CLAUDE.md` or `AGENTS.md` in this repo) — flagging in case you want to add them later or accept the dead links for now.

The usual ways to mirror Git data between remotes: a local `git clone --mirror` followed by `git push --mirror` turns a remote-to-remote movement into a local storage and bandwidth problem; host-specific migration features aren't portable across providers; and shell scripts around `git fetch` and `git push` usually lack planning, explicit policy, and machine-readable output.

`git-sync` is meant to be the missing middle layer: a provider-agnostic, remote-to-remote primitive that streams packs directly source-to-target when possible, front-loads validation, exposes typed JSON output, and covers both empty-target bootstrap and incremental sync with one tool. It's the right fit when relay is common enough to be the normal case rather than an exceptional optimization, and when avoiding persistent local repo storage is itself an operational advantage.

> Mirroring Git data between remotes usually means a local mirror clone followed by a mirror push. That's fine for small repos but turns a remote-to-remote operation into a local storage problem at scale, and shell glue around `git fetch` / `git push` tends to skip planning and structured output.
>
> `git-sync` fills that gap. It streams source packs directly into target `receive-pack` when it can, plans every action before pushing, and emits typed JSON for automation.

**B — Three sentences**
> Mirroring Git data between remotes usually means a local `clone --mirror` followed by `push --mirror`, which turns a remote-to-remote operation into a local storage problem. `git-sync` moves refs directly remote-to-remote, streaming source packs into target `receive-pack` without local materialization. It plans actions before pushing and emits JSON for automation.

**C — Two sentences, driest**
> `git-sync` moves refs between two Git remotes without a local mirror clone in the middle. It streams packs directly source-to-target when possible, plans actions before pushing, and outputs JSON for automation.

**D — Bullet form, even tighter**

>
> - streams source packs directly into target `receive-pack` when possible
> - plans every action before pushing
> - emits typed JSON for automation

result.output:
1	# git-sync
2	
3	`git-sync` mirrors refs from a source remote to a target remote without creating a local checkout. It uses an in-memory `go-git` object store and talks smart HTTP directly:
4	
5	- `info/refs` ref advertisement for source and target
6	- `upload-pack` fetch from source with target tip hashes advertised as `have`
7	- `receive-pack` push to target with explicit ref update commands and a streamed packfile
8	
9	That keeps the target side incremental without fetching target objects into the local process first.
10	
11	## Why This Exists
12	
13	The usual ways to mirror Git data between remotes are awkward at exactly the layer operators tend to need: a local `git clone --mirror` followed by `git push --mirror` turns a remote-to-remote movement into a local storage and bandwidth problem; host-specific migration features aren't portable across providers; and shell scripts around `git fetch` and `git push` usually lack planning, explicit policy, and machine-readable output.
14	
15	`git-sync` is meant to be the missing middle layer: a provider-agnostic, remote-to-remote primitive that streams packs directly source-to-target when possible, front-loads validation, exposes typed JSON output, and covers both empty-target bootstrap and incremental sync with one tool. It's the right fit when relay is common enough to be the normal case rather than an exceptional optimization, and when avoiding persistent local repo storage is itself an operational advantage.
16	
17	For when to use it (and when not), how it compares to local-clone services, and the operation-mode and transfer-mode model, see [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md).
18	
19	## Commands
20	
21	The command surface is:
22	
23	- `git-sync probe`: inspect a source remote, and optionally a target remote
24	- `git-sync plan`: compute source-to-target ref actions without pushing, with `--mode sync|replicate`
25	- `git-sync sync`: execute the planned changes against the target
26	- `git-sync replicate`: execute source-authoritative relay-only replication against the target
27	
28	`sync` auto-selects the bootstrap relay path on empty targets, so the same command covers initial seeding and ongoing sync.
29	
30	## Library API
31	
32	`git-sync` is also a Go library. Use `pkg/gitsync` for the stable embedding surface (`Probe`, `Plan`, `Sync`, `Replicate`, typed results, auth and HTTP injection). `pkg/gitsync/unstable` exposes advanced controls (`Bootstrap`, `Fetch`, batching knobs, heap measurement) and is intentionally not stable. See [docs/embedding.md](docs/embedding.md) for the worker-oriented guide.
33	
34	## Quick Start
35	
36	```bash
37	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
38	  --source-token "$GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN" \
39	  --target-token "$GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN" \
40	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
41	  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
42	```
43	
44	## Examples
45	
46	Plan a sync without pushing anything:
47	
48	```bash
49	go run ./cmd/git-sync plan \
50	  --stats \
51	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
52	  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
53	```
54	
55	Plan a source-authoritative replication without pushing anything:
56	
57	```bash
58	go run ./cmd/git-sync plan \
59	  --mode replicate \
60	  --stats \
61	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
62	  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
63	```
64	
65	Execute relay-only replication that overwrites differing managed refs and fails instead of materializing locally:
66	
67	```bash
68	go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
69	  --stats \
70	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
71	  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
72	```
73	
74	If `replicate` cannot use relay against the target, it fails and tells you to rerun with `sync`.
75	
76	For very large initial migrations, add `--target-max-pack-bytes` to split the initial pack into multiple relay batches with temporary refs. `sync` auto-bootstraps on empty targets, so the same flag works without invoking a separate command:
77	
78	```bash
79	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
80	  --target-max-pack-bytes 536870912 \
81	  --protocol v2 \
82	  -v \
83	  <source-url> \
84	  <target-url>
85	```
86	
87	Add `--measure-memory` to any command to sample elapsed time and Go heap usage:
88	
89	```bash
90	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
91	  --measure-memory \
92	  --json \
93	  <source-url> \
94	  <target-url>
95	```
96	
97	## Sync Behavior
98	
99	`sync` auto-selects the bootstrap relay path when the target has no managed refs and the run matches bootstrap semantics. It also has a narrow incremental relay path for safe fast-forward updates that streams the source pack directly into target `receive-pack` without local materialization. Updates that aren't relay-eligible (force, prune, deletes, tag retargets) fall back to a materialized path bounded by `--materialized-max-objects`. See [docs/incremental-relay.md](docs/incremental-relay.md) and [docs/bootstrap.md](docs/bootstrap.md) for details.
100	
101	Sync specific branches:
102	
103	```bash
104	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
105	  --branch main,release \
106	  --source-token "$GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN" \
107	  --target-token "$GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN" \
108	  <source-url> \
109	  <target-url>
110	```
111	
112	Map a source branch to a different target branch:
113	
114	```bash
115	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
116	  --map main:stable \
117	  <source-url> \
118	  <target-url>
119	```
120	
121	Mirror tags and prune managed target refs that disappeared from source:
122	
123	```bash
124	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
125	  --tags \
126	  --prune \
127	  <source-url> \
128	  <target-url>
129	```
130	
131	Force source-side protocol v2:
132	
133	```bash
134	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
135	  --protocol v2 \
136	  <source-url> \
137	  <target-url>
138	```
139	
140	Probe a source remote without pushing anything:
141	
142	```bash
143	go run ./cmd/git-sync probe \
144	  --stats \
145	  --tags \
146	  --protocol auto \
147	  <source-url>
148	```
149	
150	Probe both source and target remotes to inspect source fetch capabilities and target `receive-pack` capabilities:
151	
152	```bash
153	go run ./cmd/git-sync probe \
154	  --stats \
155	  <source-url> \
156	  <target-url>
157	```
158	
159	Dry run:
160	
161	```bash
162	go run ./cmd/git-sync plan --stats <source-url> <target-url>
163	```
164	
165	## JSON Output
166	
167	Add `--json` to `probe`, `plan`, or `sync` to emit machine-readable output instead of the default text format.
168	
169	The JSON interface is intentionally stable:
170	
171	- keys use `camelCase`
172	- refs and hashes are serialized as strings, not raw byte arrays
173	- `probe` returns top-level keys such as `sourceUrl`, `targetUrl`, `protocol`, `refPrefixes`, `sourceCapabilities`, `targetCapabilities`, `refs`, and `stats`
174	- `plan` and `sync` return top-level keys such as `plans`, `pushed`, `skipped`, `blocked`, `deleted`, `dryRun`, `protocol`, and `stats`, and also expose `relay`, `relayMode`, `relayReason`, `batching`, `batchCount`, `plannedBatchCount`, and `tempRefs`
175	- each item in `plans` includes stable string fields such as `branch`, `sourceRef`, `targetRef`, `sourceHash`, `targetHash`, `kind`, `action`, and `reason`
176	
177	## Auth
178	
179	For GitHub and similar providers, use basic auth with a token as the password.
180	
181	Auth is resolved in this order:
182	
183	- explicit CLI flags
184	- `GITSYNC_*` environment variables
185	- local `git credential fill` helper lookup for `http` and `https` remotes
186	- anonymous access
187	
188	Relevant variables:
189	
190	- `GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN`
191	- `GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN`
192	- `GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME` default: `git`
193	- `GITSYNC_TARGET_USERNAME` default: `git`
194	
195	Bearer auth is also available:
196	
197	- `GITSYNC_SOURCE_BEARER_TOKEN`
198	- `GITSYNC_TARGET_BEARER_TOKEN`
199	
200	That means local testing against a dummy GitHub repo can reuse your regular Git credential helper setup without passing tokens on every command.
201	
202	## Protocol Notes
203	
204	- Source-side discovery and fetch can use protocol v2 when supported; push stays on the existing v1 `receive-pack` path. `--protocol auto` tries v2 first and falls back to v1; `--protocol v2` requires the source to negotiate v2.
205	- Source fetch advertises current target tip hashes as `have`, so reruns download less when source and target already share history.
206	- Branches are updated only when the target tip is an ancestor of the source tip, unless `--force` is set. Tags are immutable by default; retargeting an existing tag requires `--force`. If `--prune` is set, managed target refs that are absent on source are deleted.
207	- `plan` never pushes. If `sync` finds blocked refs, it exits non-zero before pushing anything.
208	- `--stats` adds per-service request, byte, want, have, and command counters to the output.
209	
210	For the deeper protocol-level walkthrough (smart HTTP, pkt-line, capability negotiation, sideband stripping, relay framing), see [docs/protocol.md](docs/protocol.md).
211	
212	## Testing
213	
214	Default suite:
215	
216	```bash
217	env GOCACHE=/tmp/go-build go test ./...
218	```
219	
220	Extended and environment-specific test instructions are in [docs/testing.md](docs/testing.md).
221	
222	## Documentation
223	
224	- [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) — product rationale, package layout, operation modes vs transfer modes, memory model
225	- [docs/protocol.md](docs/protocol.md) — smart HTTP, pkt-line, capability negotiation, sideband, relay framing
226	- [docs/bootstrap.md](docs/bootstrap.md) — empty-target relay
227	- [docs/bootstrap-batching.md](docs/bootstrap-batching.md) — checkpoint batching for very large initial migrations
228	- [docs/incremental-relay.md](docs/incremental-relay.md) — narrow relay fast path inside `sync`
229	- [docs/replicate.md](docs/replicate.md) — source-authoritative relay-only overwrite mode
230	- [docs/embedding.md](docs/embedding.md) — using `git-sync` as a Go library
231	- [docs/benchmarking.md](docs/benchmarking.md) — `git-sync-bench` usage
232	- [docs/testing.md](docs/testing.md) — test suites and integration coverage
233	
234	## Contributing
235	
236	See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md), [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md), and [CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md).
237

19	## Commands
20	
21	The command surface is:
22	
23	- `git-sync sync`: mirror source refs into the target
24	- `git-sync replicate`: overwrite target refs to match source via relay, and fail rather than materialize locally
25	
26	`sync` automatically bootstraps an empty target, so the same command covers initial seeding and ongoing sync.
27	
28	## Library API
29	
30	`git-sync` is also a Go library. Use `pkg/gitsync` for the stable embedding surface (`Probe`, `Plan`, `Sync`, `Replicate`, typed results, auth and HTTP injection). `pkg/gitsync/unstable` exposes advanced controls (`Bootstrap`, `Fetch`, batching knobs, heap measurement) and is not stable. See [docs/embedding.md](docs/embedding.md) for the worker-oriented guide.
31	
32	## Quick Start
33	
34	```bash
35	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
36	  --source-token "$GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN" \
37	  --target-token "$GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN" \
38	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
39	  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
40	```
41	
42	## Examples
43	
44	Run a replication that overwrites differing target refs, and fail instead of falling back to local materialization:
45	
46	```bash
47	go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
48	  --stats \
49	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
50	  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
51	```
52	
53	If `replicate` cannot use relay against the target, it fails and tells you to rerun with `sync`.
54	
55	For very large initial migrations, add `--target-max-pack-bytes` to split the initial pack into multiple relay batches with temporary refs. The same flag works on `sync`, since `sync` auto-bootstraps on empty targets:
56	
57	```bash
58	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
59	  --target-max-pack-bytes 536870912 \
60	  --protocol v2 \
61	  -v \
62	  <source-url> \
63	  <target-url>
64	```
65	
66	Add `--measure-memory` to any command to sample elapsed time and Go heap usage:
67	
68	```bash
69	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
70	  --measure-memory \
71	  --json \
72	  <source-url> \
73	  <target-url>
74	```
75	
76	## Sync Behavior
77	
78	`sync` picks the bootstrap relay path automatically when the target is empty. For non-empty targets, safe fast-forward updates also use a relay path that streams the source pack directly into target `receive-pack` without local materialization. Anything not relay-eligible (force, prune, deletes, tag retargets) falls back to a materialized path bounded by `--materialized-max-objects`. See [docs/incremental-relay.md](docs/incremental-relay.md) and [docs/bootstrap.md](docs/bootstrap.md) for details.
79	
80	Sync specific branches:
81	
82	```bash
83	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
84	  --branch main,release \
85	  --source-token "$GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN" \
86	  --target-token "$GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN" \
87	  <source-url> \
88	  <target-url>
89	```
90	
91	Map a source branch to a different target branch:
92	
93	```bash
94	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
95	  --map main:stable \
96	  <source-url> \
97	  <target-url>
98	```
99	
100	Mirror tags and prune managed target refs that disappeared from source:
101	
102	```bash
103	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
104	  --tags \
105	  --prune \
106	  <source-url> \
107	  <target-url>
108	```
109	
110	Force source-side protocol v2:
111	
112	```bash
113	go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
114	  --protocol v2 \
115	  <source-url> \
116	  <target-url>
117	```
118	
119	## JSON Output
120	
121	Add `--json` to `sync` or `replicate` to emit machine-readable output instead of the default text format.
122	
123	The JSON interface is stable:
124	
125	- keys use `camelCase`
126	- refs and hashes are serialized as strings, not raw byte arrays
127	- top-level keys include `plans`, `pushed`, `skipped`, `blocked`, `deleted`, `dryRun`, `protocol`, and `stats`, plus `relay`, `relayMode`, `relayReason`, `batching`, `batchCount`, `plannedBatchCount`, and `tempRefs`
128	- each item in `plans` includes stable string fields such as `branch`, `sourceRef`, `targetRef`, `sourceHash`, `targetHash`, `kind`, `action`, and `reason`
129	
130	## Auth
131	
132	For GitHub and similar providers, use basic auth with a token as the password.
133	
134	Auth is resolved in this order:
135	
136	- explicit CLI flags
137	- `GITSYNC_*` environment variables
138	- local `git credential fill` helper lookup for `http` and `https` remotes

content.text:
Reading the actual `relay.go` confirms the issue: incremental relay rejects `NoThin` (line 67), replicate explicitly tolerates it (line 17 comment). My docs had it backwards in three places. Fixing now along with the CONTRIBUTING.md stale refs and the README wording.

**Fix 1: `incremental-relay.md` — invert the `no-thin` claim**

input.old_string:
- target advertises `no-thin` on `receive-pack`

`no-thin` is the load-bearing capability. The relayed pack must be self-contained because git-sync's source fetch never requests the `thin-pack` capability, so the target can apply the pack directly without resolving deltas against existing target objects. See [protocol.md](protocol.md) for the framing details.
input.new_string:
- target does **not** advertise `no-thin` on `receive-pack`

The `no-thin` check is conservative: when a target advertises `no-thin`, it is signalling that it does not support thin packs. `git-sync`'s source fetch never requests the `thin-pack` capability, so the relayed pack is always self-contained and would in fact be safe for a `no-thin` target — but the incremental relay planner skips this case as a safety margin and falls back to the materialized path instead. (`replicate` is less conservative and tolerates `no-thin` targets explicitly; see [replicate.md](replicate.md).) See [protocol.md](protocol.md#why-no-thin-matters) for the framing details.

input.old_string:
## Eligibility

`replicate` requires the same relay-eligibility conditions as the incremental relay path inside `sync`:

- target advertises `no-thin` on `receive-pack`. The relayed pack must be self-contained because git-sync's source fetch never requests the `thin-pack` capability — see [protocol.md](protocol.md) for the framing detail.
- the source pack can be streamed directly into target `receive-pack` without local object decoding.

If those conditions are not met, `replicate` aborts before any push.
input.new_string:
## Eligibility

`replicate` requires that the source pack can be streamed directly into target `receive-pack` without local object decoding. Unlike the incremental relay path inside `sync`, `replicate` explicitly tolerates targets that advertise `no-thin`: the source pack is always self-contained because `git-sync` never requests `thin-pack` on `upload-pack`, so the relayed pack is safe regardless of `no-thin` advertisement. See [protocol.md](protocol.md#why-no-thin-matters) for the framing detail.

If a relay-safe path is not available against the target (for example, target capabilities cannot be discovered), `replicate` aborts before any push.

input.old_string:
### Why `no-thin` matters

A "thin pack" is one that contains delta objects whose base is not in the pack itself. The receiver is expected to resolve those bases against existing target objects. Thin packs are smaller on the wire but require the receiver to reach into the existing object database during indexing.

For relay, this is incompatible: the target must accept the source pack as-is without trying to resolve external bases. `git-sync` ensures this by:

1. Never requesting the `thin-pack` capability on `upload-pack`. The source therefore produces a self-contained pack.
2. Refusing to use the relay path unless the target advertises `no-thin` on `receive-pack`. That's the target's signal that it will accept and verify a non-thin pack.
input.new_string:
### Why `no-thin` matters

A "thin pack" is one that contains delta objects whose base is not in the pack itself. The receiver is expected to resolve those bases against existing target objects. Thin packs are smaller on the wire but require the receiver to reach into the existing object database during indexing. When a `receive-pack` server advertises `no-thin`, it is signalling that it does not support thin packs — the client must send a self-contained pack.

`git-sync` always sends self-contained packs because it never requests the `thin-pack` capability on `upload-pack` (see the comment in `internal/gitproto/fetch.go`). That means the relayed pack is safe for both kinds of target. The relay paths still make different choices given the target's advertisement:

- **Replicate** explicitly tolerates `no-thin` and proceeds with relay. The comment in `internal/planner/relay.go` documents the reasoning: source never sends thin-pack, so the relayed pack is self-contained and safe.
- **Incremental relay** inside `sync` is conservative: it skips `no-thin` targets and falls back to the materialized path. This is a safety-margin choice; it could in principle be relaxed using the same argument as replicate.
- **Bootstrap** does not consult `no-thin` — empty-target relay is allowed regardless.

---

## Creating an Agent


Entire supports two ways to create agents:

### 1. Claude Code Agent Personas (Markdown)

These are markdown files that define specialized behaviors for Claude Code (e.g., developer, reviewer, etc.).

- **Location:** `.claude/agents/`
- **Structure:**
   ```markdown
   ---
   name: my-agent
   description: What this agent does
   model: opus
   color: blue
   ---

   # Agent Name
   You are a **[Role]** with expertise in [domain].

   ## Core Principles
   - Principle 1
   - Principle 2

   ## Process
   1. Step 1
   2. Step 2

   ## Output Format
   How to structure responses...
   ```
- **To invoke:** Create a matching command in `.claude/commands/` that spawns the agent via the Task tool.
- **Examples:**
   - `.claude/agents/dev.md` - TDD Developer
   - `.claude/agents/reviewer.md` - Code Reviewer

### 2. Coding Agent Integrations (Go)

These are Go implementations that integrate Entire with different AI coding tools (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, Cursor, Factory AI Droid, Copilot CLI, etc.) using the Agent abstraction layer.

- **Location:** `cmd/entire/cli/agent/`
- **Steps:**
   1. Implement the `Agent` interface in `agent/agent.go`
   2. Register your agent in the agent registry
   3. Add setup and hook configuration as needed
   4. Ensure session and checkpoint tracking is handled per the abstraction
- **Reference:** See [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for architecture and code examples.

---

**Which should I use?**

- Use a persona markdown agent if you want to create a new role or workflow for Claude Code.
- Use a coding agent integration if you want to add support for a new AI coding tool or extend agent capabilities in the CLI.

---

## Submitting a Pull Request

content.text: **Fix 5: README "command surface" wording**

input.old_string:
## Commands

The command surface is:

- `git-sync sync`: mirror source refs into the target
- `git-sync replicate`: overwrite target refs to match source via relay, and fail rather than materialize locally

`sync` automatically bootstraps an empty target, so the same command covers initial seeding and ongoing sync. To preview what would happen without pushing, run `git-sync plan` — it takes the same flags as `sync`, and `--mode replicate` previews a `replicate` run.
input.new_string:
## Commands

The main commands are:

- `git-sync sync`: mirror source refs into the target
- `git-sync replicate`: overwrite target refs to match source via relay, and fail rather than materialize locally

`sync` automatically bootstraps an empty target, so the same command covers initial seeding and ongoing sync. To preview what would happen without pushing, run `git-sync plan` — it takes the same flags as `sync`, and `--mode replicate` previews a `replicate` run.

Additional commands (`bootstrap`, `probe`, `fetch`) and advanced flags are available through `git-sync --help` and the [`pkg/gitsync/unstable`](pkg/gitsync/unstable) library surface; they are not part of the recommended public surface.

content.text:
All `no-thin` claims now match the code:
- replicate tolerates regardless
- incremental relay rejects (conservative)
- bootstrap doesn't consult
- protocol.md explains the divergence

**Summary of fixes**

`no-thin` correctness:
- `docs/incremental-relay.md` — eligibility flipped (target must NOT advertise `no-thin`); explanatory paragraph rewritten to match the conservative skip in `internal/planner/relay.go:67`
- `docs/replicate.md` — dropped the false `no-thin`-required claims in three places; rewrote eligibility paragraph to match the explicit tolerate in `internal/planner/relay.go:14-32`
- `docs/protocol.md` — rewrote the `Why no-thin matters` section to explain that the source pack is always self-contained and the three relay paths make different choices given the target advertisement (replicate tolerates, incremental skips conservatively, bootstrap doesn't consult); fixed the corresponding common-failures bullet and the introductory framing in §4
- `docs/architecture.md` — tightened replicate's description from "works against targets that advertise `no-thin`" (could be misread as exclusive) to "works against targets regardless of `no-thin` advertisement"

`CONTRIBUTING.md` stale refs:
- `git-sync version` → `git rev-parse HEAD`
- `mise run test:integration` and `cmd/entire/cli/integration_test/` reference → replaced with the actual git-sync mise tasks (`test:ci`, `test:git-http-backend`, `test:linux-smoke`, `test:linux-smoke:batched`) and a pointer to `docs/testing.md`
- Three `[CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md)` link references → replaced with `docs/architecture.md` or `docs/testing.md` as appropriate
- `Creating an Agent` section (cli-only paths and content) → dropped entirely
- `[CLAUDE.md]` and `[AGENTS.md]` entries in `Additional Resources` → replaced with `docs/architecture.md` and `docs/testing.md`
- (Go `1.26.x` left to you, as you said)

`README.md` command surface wording:
- "The command surface is:" → "The main commands are:"
- Added a clarifying line below the existing description: *"Additional commands (`bootstrap`, `probe`, `fetch`) and advanced flags are available through `git-sync --help` and the [unstable library surface](docs/embedding.md). They are not part of the recommended public surface."*

All changes uncommitted on `soph/docs-update-for-oss-release`.
